From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Football League Trophy](/w/index.php?title=Football_League_Trophy&redirect=no "Football League Trophy"))

Association football tournament in England

Not to be confused with the [EFL Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup").

For the 1982–83 competition known as the Football League Trophy, see [Football League Group Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Group_Cup "Football League Group Cup").

Football tournament

EFL Trophy

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Current logo | |
| Organiser(s) | [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") |

| Region | * England * Wales |
| Teams | 64 |
| Current champions | [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United_F.C. "Peterborough United F.C.") (3rd title) |
| Most championships | [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United_F.C. "Peterborough United F.C.") (3 titles each) |
| *[2025–26 EFL Trophy](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_EFL_Trophy "2025–26 EFL Trophy")* | |

The **English Football League Trophy**, officially known as the **[Vertu](/wiki/Vertu_Motors "Vertu Motors") Trophy** for sponsorship reasons, is an annual English [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [knockout competition](/wiki/Knockout_competition "Knockout competition") open to all clubs in [EFL League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One"), [EFL League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two"), and [U-21](/wiki/Professional_Development_League "Professional Development League") teams from the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and the [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship").[[1]](#cite_note-trial-1)

Launched in the [1983–84 football season](/wiki/1983%E2%80%9384_in_English_football "1983–84 in English football") as the **Associate Members' Cup**, the competition was renamed the **Football League Trophy** in 1992 after a reorganisation following the formation of the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and again as the current *EFL Trophy* in 2016 due to The Football League changing its name to the [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League").[[1]](#cite_note-trial-1)

The current competition begins with 16 regional groups, each containing 4 teams and divided between northern and southern sections depending on the clubs' geographic locations. The top two from each group qualify for the knockout stages before the two winners meet in late March or early April in the final at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium"). Some [Midlands](/wiki/The_Midlands "The Midlands") and [East Anglian](/wiki/East_Anglia "East Anglia") clubs fluctuate between the north and south each season for every draw. Other details have varied over the years, including in some years inviting clubs from the [National League](/wiki/National_League_(division) "National League (division)"), and holding a round-robin group stage before moving into knockout rounds.

The most successful clubs are [Bristol City](/wiki/Bristol_City_F.C. "Bristol City F.C.") and [Peterborough United](/wiki/Peterborough_United "Peterborough United"), who have lifted the trophy three times each. The current winners are Peterborough United, who beat [Birmingham City](/wiki/Birmingham_City_F.C. "Birmingham City F.C.") 2–0 in the [2025 final](/wiki/2025_EFL_Trophy_final "2025 EFL Trophy final"), becoming the only club to retain the title after having won the [2024 final](/wiki/2024_EFL_Trophy_final "2024 EFL Trophy final") against [Wycombe Wanderers](/wiki/Wycombe_Wanderers_F.C. "Wycombe Wanderers F.C.").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The competition was created in 1983, and was open to Third and Fourth Division [Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League") clubs (levels 3 and 4 of the English football pyramid). The competition was introduced to give clubs in the lower divisions of the Football League a more realistic opportunity to play at Wembley.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It replaced the [Football League Group Cup](/wiki/Football_League_Group_Cup "Football League Group Cup") (known as the Football League Trophy in its final season), in which some (but not all) Third and Fourth Division teams had competed. The first winners of the competition were [Bournemouth](/wiki/AFC_Bournemouth "AFC Bournemouth").

In 1992 the tournament rebranded as the *Football League Trophy*, coinciding with a reorganisation following the decision of the [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") clubs at the time to form the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 2016 the competition rebranded to the current *EFL Trophy* after The Football League rebranding as the [English Football League](/wiki/English_Football_League "English Football League").[[4]](#cite_note-4) [The first season under the new name](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_EFL_Trophy "2016–17 EFL Trophy") saw 16 [Category One](/wiki/Elite_Player_Performance_Plan "Elite Player Performance Plan") academies of [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") and [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") clubs join the competition.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In 2023 participating clubs received a £20,000 participation fee, with £10,000 per victory and £5,000 per draw in the group stage, and increasing prizes for the knockout matches up to £100,000 for winning the final.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Formats

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Formats")]

### Associate Members' Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Associate Members' Cup")]

#### 1983 to 1985

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1983 to 1985")]

The 48 clubs of the [Third](/wiki/Football_League_Third_Division "Football League Third Division") and [Fourth Divisions](/wiki/Football_League_Fourth_Division "Football League Fourth Division") were split into North and South sections of 24 teams each. The first round had 12 knockout ties in each section, and the second had six. In each section, the two second-round losers with the 'narrowest' defeats were reprieved and joined the six other clubs in the regional quarter-finals.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

#### 1985 to 1992

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1985 to 1992")]

For [the 1985–86 edition](/wiki/1985%E2%80%9386_Football_League_Trophy "1985–86 Football League Trophy"), 8 three-team groups were introduced in each of the two sections. Teams played one home and one away game and the group winners proceeded to the regional knockout stages.[[8]](#cite_note-8) This was modified in [the following season](/wiki/1986%E2%80%9387_Football_League_Trophy "1986–87 Football League Trophy"), with two teams qualifying from each group, resulting in an additional 'round of 16' knockout stage in each section.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Football League Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Football League Trophy")]

#### 1992 to 1996

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1992 to 1996")]

For a number of seasons in the early to mid-1990s, the competition ran with seven 3-team groups, two teams in each section receiving a [bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)") into the knockout stages. This was a direct result of the folding of [Aldershot](/wiki/Aldershot_F.C. "Aldershot F.C.") and [Maidstone United](/wiki/Maidstone_United_F.C._(1897) "Maidstone United F.C. (1897)") necessitating a reorganisation of the competition to accommodate fewer than 48 teams in the tournament.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

#### 1996 to 2000

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 1996 to 2000")]

The group phase was abolished for the [1996–97 edition](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Football_League_Trophy "1996–97 Football League Trophy"). The regional sections were retained and 8 teams in each section received a bye to the second round.

#### 2000 to 2006

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2000 to 2006")]

For [the 2000–01 season](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Football_League_Trophy "2000–01 Football League Trophy"), 8 [teams in level 5 (step 1) of the football pyramid](/wiki/National_League_(English_football) "National League (English football)") were invited to participate in the tournament, resulting in 12 ties in each of the north/south sections in the first round, with only four teams in each section receiving a bye into the second round. The number of invitees increased to 12 from [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Football_League_Trophy "2002–03 Football League Trophy"), resulting in 14 first-round ties, and two teams in each regional section gaining a bye into the second round.

#### 2006 to 2016

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2006 to 2016")]

For the [2006–07 tournament](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Football_League_Trophy "2006–07 Football League Trophy") onward, the Conference team invitations were abolished. This resulted in the format reverting to 8 first-round matches in each section, and 8 sides receiving byes to the second round.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Due to a number of clubs fielding weakened teams in the competition, a rule was introduced which required a minimum of six first-team regulars to be named in the starting lineup.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### EFL Trophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: EFL Trophy")]

#### 2016 to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: 2016 to present")]

64 teams enter from Round One; all 48 teams from levels three and four of the football pyramid ([League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") and [League Two](/wiki/EFL_League_Two "EFL League Two")), along with 16 category 1 Premier League and [EFL Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") academy/under-21 sides. 16 regional groups of four teams split evenly 8 northern and 8 southern. Each of the groups contains one academy team. The top two from each group progress to the knockout stages; up to and including the quarter-final the tournament remains regionalised before becoming an open draw from the semi-finals onwards.[[14]](#cite_note-format-14)

During the group phase, if the scores are level at the end of the match, then penalties are taken immediately without recourse to extra time. The winning team is awarded 2 points and the losing team 1 point.[[14]](#cite_note-format-14) During the knock-out phase, up to but not including the final, if the scores are level at the end of the match the winner is decided by penalties. In the final, if the scores are equal after 90 minutes an extra 30 minutes are played and if still equal the winner is then decided by penalties.[[14]](#cite_note-format-14)

## Finals

### Venue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Venue")]

The final of the EFL Trophy is currently held at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in [London](/wiki/London "London"), the English national stadium. The final in 1984 was due to be played at [the previous Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)"), but owing to damage caused to the pitch during the [Horse of the Year Show](/wiki/Horse_of_the_Year_Show "Horse of the Year Show"), it was moved to [Boothferry Park](/wiki/Boothferry_Park "Boothferry Park") in [Hull](/wiki/Kingston_upon_Hull "Kingston upon Hull").[[15]](#cite_note-15) From 2001 to 2007, during the building of the new Wembley, the Football League Trophy finals were played at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Attendances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=EFL_Trophy&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Attendances")]

The overall record attendance for the final is 85,021, set at the [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in 2019 by [Portsmouth](/wiki/Portsmouth_F.C. "Portsmouth F.C.") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."). The record attendance for the final at [the original Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium_(1923) "Wembley Stadium (1923)") was 80,841, set in the [1988 final](/wiki/1988_Football_League_Trophy_Final "1988 Football League Trophy Final") between [Wolverhampton Wanderers](/wiki/Wolverhampton_Wanderers_F.C. "Wolverhampton Wanderers F.C.") and [Burnley](/wiki/Burnley_F.C. "Burnley F.C.").[[19]](#cite_note-19) The record attendance for the final at the [Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium") in [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") was 59,024, set in the [2007 final](/wiki/2007_Football_League_Trophy_Final "2007 Football League Trophy Final") between [Bristol Rovers](/wiki/Bristol_Rovers_F.C. "Bristol Rovers F.C.") and [Doncaster Rovers](/wiki/Doncaster_Rovers_F.C. "Doncaster Rovers F.C.").[[20]](#cite_note-20) The 2020 and 2021 finals were played behind closed doors, but clubs raised money for charity by selling supporters virtual tickets.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
